segments.text: Okay, so, um, hello and welcome in
and thank you for making the time to come into Parliament.
Um, would be great if, well each group
of you will have 15 minutes to present.
I do welcome that you leave some time for questions
and answers and members will be very keen to ask
for some specific examples as well.
Uh, I would recommend that you introduce yourself
so we can put a name, um, to your face
and it will just help with the dialogue. Welcome.
Thank you Kiara. Um, I'm Dr. Alexandra Brand Wheeler.
I'm Chief Executive of Master Electricians.
Um, and I have with me today my, uh, board chair RI Ash.
I'm Warren Willett, I'm the president
of New Zealand Specs, electrical Inspectors Association,
and I have with me my educator,
David Ty here would also have Mike Speaker as well.
And Mark Harris, Uh, sorry. He is on there.
Cool. Okay. Thank you. Okay.
You're welcome to make, uh, introductory remarks.
Thank you. So I'll, I'll lead, uh, with this statement
and then I'll hand over to the electrical
inspectors to add their bit.
So the Gazette Notice 2025
SL 4 1 9 1, which I'll just refer to
as the GC notice going forward, expands
and restructures the licensing into 41 classes
of new endorsed high risk work,
all requiring separate licenses.
We fully support regulating high risk work competency,
but this notice has produced an unworkable licensing regime
with extreme adverse consequences.
While the current economic conditions have muted the
immediate impact of our concerns.
The expected economic recovery combined
with potential immigration adjustments over the next 18
months, we'll rapidly intensify workforce demands,
underscoring the burning need to be prepared for
what is coming as pressure accelerates.
Firstly, the notice resets licensing for some high risk
and emerging areas, hazardous areas,
medical patient treatment areas,
and mains, parallel generation systems such as solar
and battery storage.
Yet teaching guidance has not kept up to speed.
Assessment materials for the endorsements is absent.
Therefore, training organizations cannot
actually develop courses to deliver this capable workforce.
This creates a compliance catch 22 situation.
Workers need the endorsement to work legally
but cannot obtain the endorsement
because the pathway is not available
or requires prior work in the environment.
The second issue we have is that the notice is inconsistent
with the key statutory constraints
under section 87 B and C of the Electricity Act 1992.
The electrical Workers registration Board must not
unnecessarily restrict licensing
or impose undue cost on workers or the public.
In practice, the regime has created situations
where electricians now require multiple licenses
to do work historically covered by one license.
Each attracting fees included repeated application fees
and renewals every two years plus time away from paid work
for repeated courses.
The board has provided no clear pricing
or renewal transparency for multi licensed renewals
and course prerequisites.
This also creates unfair advantage
to the larger electrical firms from the sole trader,
the rural electrician whereby the sole trader has onerous
licensing costs and added to them is difficult to obtain.
The third issue we have with this is
that the con consultation was inadequate for a change
of this magnitude.
Section 88 requires consultation on the proposed
notice contents.
The earlier process in terms of consultation
captured only about 0.2% of the workforce
relied heavily on online survey mechanisms
and did not meaningfully engage representative
bodies like ourselves.
Critically what industry was shown
and could comment on an endorsement approach on a primary
license was not what was implemented.
The Gazette outcome delivered an expanded 41 class structure
where every new endorsement requires a separate
and additional license.
Targeted consultation in 2025 ran for only two weeks
and key industry feedback was not reflected.
The notice was released on the 1st of August, 2025
and took effect on the 4th of August, 2025,
three days later, leaving no realistic implementation time.
The fourth issue we have is that the notice calls
for explanation because it appears to leave routine safety
critical work or unauthorized, unclear
or unauthorized limits of work for electricians
and inspectors can find their prescribed electrical work
to work on installations, works,
or appliances, yet prescribed electrical work.
As defined in the electrical safety regulations includes a
specific and additional form of prescribed electrical work,
which is not work on an installation.
This is known as con connecting fittings to a power supply.
The practical consequences of failure
to authorize this is serious.
The electrical safety regulations 2010
prescribed the electrical safety procedures
for verifying the electrical safety
of this additional connection,
additional connection work which has been carried out
by representatives of the installation owner
or by a lines company without authorization from the
EWRB or the Electrical Workers Registration Board.
To make the connection to our power supply.
Electrical workers cannot lawfully verify the completed
installation is safe for consumers
to use in the manner in which the law requires
and that prevents consumers from safely
and lawfully using the installations they're paying for.
The scope of the board's powers is limited
to work defined in schedule one
of the electricity safety regulations 2010.
The government's risk management analysis has resulted in
only two locations being prescribed in the schedule
to enable the board's licensing, licensing intervention.
These areas are hazardous areas in commercial
and industrial locations and mines.
The board has created other licenses tied
to other locations.
The EWRB has Gazette notices that are ultra varies
to the scope of the prescribed electrical work and
therefore the board's delegated powers under
the electricity act.
When the EWRB was set up
and commenced in 1993, the board determined
that the competency it was created
to ensure was electrical safety competence.
The singular focus left technical competencies
and the skills required to handle the challenges posed
by different electrical applications to be achieved
through a qualification
or a credential path rather than by the licensing path.
The qualification
and credential path can better be determined
by industry, not the board.
Now Alexandra, I just want you
to know we are about halfway through the submission for
the two groups together.
Okay. But also we have members are keen to ask questions.
Okay. Do you wanna give us like a very quick summary? Sure.
Look, I mean I think we've outlined what we think are the,
the legal issues around that.
To be honest, the real world impacts on industry workforce
cap capacity and capability

Our workforce capacity is being restrained just when
New Zealand needs electricians

it's not coming, it's here.
Workers cannot lawfully upskill in pathways given the issue
that we've got around licensing.
So we are here today because we feel our electrical
compliance system is siloed, it's dysfunctional
and obstructive to the growth
of our national productivity agenda.
I'm happy to hand over to Warren to add his

you.
And we do have three members
who are interested on Christians welcome.
Sure. Welcome. Thank you.

to public safety via secondary legislation changes.
We are frustrated with a number of shortcomings.

electricity and who has never read the notice
that he published an implementation
of 41 classes of license.
When we ask for endorsements as add-ons
with continuous professional development, I do the course
get endorsed as competent
and complete an online assessment every year to keep up

Not having competency guidelines and assessment material
before the regulatory changes come into effect for industry
to look at and critique
and the lack of proper industry consultation.
Gazette notices cherry picking groups whilst energy safety
consults in-house with one person
deciding the outcome is hardly democratic or professionally.
P peer reviewed. Uh, we used to have a ministry of energy
as a standalone government department that could be better,
that could better integrate the breadth
of controls required.
But this portfolio has been absorbed into enb
where the minister of energy is now subject
outside technical electrical policy from only one source.
There were good reasons for creating this former singular
entity and the restoration
of an over overarching government operation
to manage all things electrical
and gas with a was a worthy objective.
We see a number of solutions.
Firstly appoint an electrical commissioner
to oversee a new electrical system of regulation,
bring the energy safety back under MB at least
to give proper technical advice by a team of competent

Ideally return to a minister of energy would be better
appoint an electrical registrar who's from the electrical

as plumbers do have the EWOB
as all competent electrical people with the lawyers
as advisors to the board.
Make the E-W-O-B-A technical point of focus again
where the electrician can ring up somebody
with electrically technical knowledge and get an answer.
Have the technical advisors to MB a team
or a board of electrically competent people from
around New Zealand from a wide
and varied industry led electrical background.
There should never be one person
and have endorsements as add-ons
to the person's current license
and kept as continuous professional development
as technology increases
to integrate into our modern societies.
So do our legislative and regulatory demands in a concise
and accurate information disseminated to the worker tasked
with interpreting law and safety.
Therefore, legislation must be fit for purpose, easy
to understand, and not one expensive.
Adding 41 classes of license doesn't the opposite of this
and NZA regards this as not listening to industry.
Thank you. Thank you Aaron. We have two minutes to go
so maybe the members if you can prioritize

We are interested in and Member Lawrence please.
Thank you. Cheer. Thank you so much for coming.

So the EWRB is responsible for the registration
and licensing of New Zealand's
33,000 plus electrical workers.
These electrical workers operate across a wide range

The board's function and powers are set out in the
electricity Act 1992.
We act in accordance with the following principles,
the health and safety of the public,

especially in the high risk areas,
and ensuring a workable approach to registration

of emerging technologies such as solar systems.
The board operates on a part-time basis
and is primarily made up of people
who are actively involved as electrical workers.
The electrical prescribed classes
of registration for electrical workers.
Notice 2025 sets out the classes of registration
for electrical workers and the minimum standards
of those different classes.
It came into effect on the 4th of August, 2025
and was part of a wider process.
The classes were first implemented through a notice in 2023.
They had a staggered commencement from 2024 to 2026 in order
to allow for a smoother transition for electoral workers.
The 2025 notice fit into this process by addressing concerns
that have been received from industry participants,
including the bodies that have brought the current complaint
and developing and implementing the notices.
The board complied with the legislative requirements,
including obtaining ministerial approval.
It also carried out significant consult consultation
for the 2023 notice.
We ran a six week consultation
and received 85 submissions for the 2025 notice.
We undertook a targeted consultation due
to the technical nature of the amendments.
Key stakeholders who were engaged were broadly
supportive of the proposals.
We went to 12 stakeholders and received five responses.
Just like to address, uh, some of the complaint here.
The board has the power to create classes, not endorsements.
So the legislative settings do not provide for a class
of registration to carry endorsements.
The range of classes are intended
to achieve the same pur purpose within
the legislative framework.
The classes created by the board aligned with the purpose
of the ACT for the relevant guiding
principles in creating them.
The board also considered the wider regulatory scheme,
including safety regulations created by the governor.
General trainees
and supervisors can undertake some work ahead
of registration.
The ACT allows workers to gain required experience under
supervision section 76 of the act.
And as trainees through limited certificates, section 77

to be demonstrated through various forms
of training and experience.
The board also has the power

We are working on ways to streamline how

There are no undue costs for registration.
Electrical workers pay a registration fee once practicing
license fees are flat fees regardless
of the number of classes you hold.
The board may also grant exemptions under section 1 0 7
of the act in the appropriate cases

To transition into the new registration framework,
we ran a grandparent, we ran a grandparenting process so

Over 8,000 applications

Costs associated

In summary, the board feels confident

The board is confident that the notice aligns

The board is committed to continued dialogue

source_metadata.title: 2026/03/04 Regulations Review
source_metadata.description:
Complaint about the Electricity (Prescribed Classes of Registration for Electrical Workers) Gazette Notice 2025

New Zealand Electrical Inspectors Association (NZEIA)
Warren Willets, President of NZEIA
David Kite, NZEIA

Master Electricians
Daniel Jone, Head of Marketing & Engagement
Alexandra Vranyac-Wheeler, Chief Executive
Omri Ash, Board Chair

Electrical Workers Registration Board
Russell Keys, Presiding Member
Thomas Wiseman, Deputy Presiding Member
Truc Tran, Member
Lucy Wright, Member
